The Teacher—the wealthy, wise King in Jerusalem—conducts an experiment on himself. He grants his desires everything they demand: wine, entertainment, monumental building projects, servants, treasures, music, and sexual pleasure. He withholds nothing. He does this not as a hedonist abandoning reason, but as a deliberate investigator, his mind still guiding him, trying to discover what is genuinely worthwhile in human life. The answer he reaches is devastating: it is all futile. This is not the conclusion of a depressed pessimist but of a man who obtained what most people spend their entire lives chasing—and found it empty. He surpassed everyone before him in Jerusalem, yet when he surveyed all his accumulated works, he found nothing of substance, nothing gained. That word "futility" haunts this chapter like a refrain that cannot be shaken.

What makes this chapter so penetrating is that the Teacher does not conclude his investigation with a simple "pleasure is empty." He presses deeper. He turns to examine wisdom itself—perhaps the one thing he could still trust. Wisdom is objectively better than folly; it is real as light exceeds darkness. The wise person sees, while the fool stumbles in darkness. Yet the Teacher makes a discovery that shatters even this hope: both the wise and the foolish face the same fate. Death comes for both. Both are forgotten. Both will be erased from human memory as if they never existed. This realization drives him to hatred—not of God, but of life itself as it is lived "under the sun," separated from any ultimate meaning or permanence. He hates his life's work not because it was painful, but because he must leave it all behind to someone he may never meet, someone who may squander it through folly.

Yet listen carefully to where the Teacher's investigation actually lands. He does not end in nihilism. After acknowledging the futility that surrounds human striving, he speaks a word that changes everything: there is nothing better for a man than to eat and drink and enjoy his work, and this—this simple gift—is from the hand of God. This is not hedonism; it is radical dependence. The Teacher recognizes that joy itself, the ability to find satisfaction in daily work and sustenance, is not something we generate or deserve. It comes from God. Without Him, we cannot truly enjoy anything at all. The final verse cuts to the heart of the matter: God gives wisdom, knowledge, and joy to those who please Him, while sinners are locked in the futile cycle of accumulation, building towers that will be handed to others. The difference is not in what you achieve, but in your relationship to God. All human accomplishment vanishes, but a life received as a gift from His hand—that alone has weight.

The Teacher's experiment exposes a lie we keep believing: that our efforts, our possessions, our accomplishments, our legacies will somehow matter eternally. They will not. But this is not cause for despair; it is an invitation to stop looking for ultimate meaning in the wrong place. Stop demanding that your work justify itself. Stop believing that what your hands can build or accumulate will satisfy the deepest hunger in your soul. Instead, recognize that even your ability to eat, to work, to find a moment of genuine pleasure—these are gifts from the hand of God. When you can receive your life as a gift rather than defend it as an achievement, you enter into the very joy the Teacher discovered. Everything changes when you stop trying to make something permanent in a world of endings, and instead cling to the One who alone is permanent.
